md: Two of the most important things you can do with functions: **chain them together** (composition) and **reverse them** (inverse). Both ideas show up everywhere â€” calculus, computer science, and any time you need to 'undo' a process.
**$(f \circ g)(x)$ means $f(g(x))$.** Apply $g$ first, then feed the result into $f$. Read it 'f composed with g' or 'f of g of x'.
Notice â€” **composition is not commutative.** $f \circ g \neq g \circ f$ in general.

blocks.title: Domain considerations
blocks.body: For $(f \circ g)(x)$ to make sense, $g(x)$ has to land in $f$'s domain. Always check the domain of the inner function first, then make sure its outputs are valid inputs for the outer function.

md: An **inverse function** undoes the original. If $f$ takes $x$ to $y$, then $f^{-1}$ takes $y$ back to $x$.

Not every function has an inverse. To have one, $f$ must be **one-to-one** â€” different inputs must give different outputs. Otherwise the inverse can't decide which input to send a given output back to.
**Test for one-to-one â€” the horizontal line test.** If every horizontal line crosses the graph of $f$ at most once, $f$ is one-to-one and has an inverse.
### How to find an inverse algebraically
Why swap? Because the inverse takes outputs back to inputs â€” so its 'input' is the original's output, and its 'output' is the original's input.

blocks.title: Geometric picture â€” reflection
blocks.body: The graph of $f^{-1}$ is the **reflection** of the graph of $f$ across the line $y = x$. If $(a, b)$ is on $f$, then $(b, a)$ is on $f^{-1}$. Plot both â€” they look like mirror images of each other across the diagonal.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: **Composition**: $(f \circ g)(x) = f(g(x))$, apply inner function first, not commutative. **Inverse**: $f^{-1}$ undoes $f$. Find by swapping $x$ and $y$ and solving. Inverse exists only for one-to-one functions. Geometrically, inverses reflect across $y = x$.
